Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent study routine, honestly. I'd block out about four hours most days, usually in the afternoon after my morning classes. I'd go through the reading materials first, take notes in my notebook, then watch any videos assigned for that week. I liked having a schedule because it made me feel like I was on top of things, even when the content was getting harder to understand.
Q2: I really preferred sticking to regular times each week. Sundays and Wednesdays were my heavy study days where I'd knock out most of the reading and assignments. Sometimes deadlines would throw me off and I'd have to cram more into certain days, but I always tried to get back to my routine as quickly as possible. The consistency helped me feel less anxious about falling behind.
Q3: Usually I logged in because it was just part of my daily habit—like checking email or something. I'd open the LMS first thing when I sat down to study to see if there were any announcements or new materials posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check discussion boards or see if anyone replied to my posts. It became automatic after a few weeks.
Q4: Yeah, definitely. Around week three or four, logging into the LMS just became something I did without thinking about it. I set it as my browser homepage, which helped a lot. I'd check it multiple times a day, even if I wasn't actively studying, just to stay updated. It felt weird if I went a whole day without logging in at least once.
Q5: The reading materials and discussion forums were probably the most useful for me. I learn best by reading and writing, so being able to download PDFs and take notes worked well with how my brain processes information. The discussion posts also helped because writing out my thoughts forced me to actually think through the concepts. The videos were okay, but I retained more from reading.
Q6: I'll admit I kind of skimmed through some of the supplementary videos and optional practice quizzes. They felt like extras that wouldn't directly impact my grade, so when I was pressed for time, those were the first things I'd skip. Looking back, maybe I should have used them more because my exam scores weren't great. I just prioritized what seemed mandatory.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was clearly connected to assignments or exams. If I could see how it would help me complete something that was being graded, I was all in. I also valued things that let me engage actively, like discussion posts where I could write out my understanding. Passive activities where I just watched or clicked through felt less productive to me.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like they were on the back burner. Required ones had this urgency attached to them—I knew I had to do them or my grade would suffer. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later rarely came. There was always something required competing for my attention, so optional activities got pushed aside even when they might have been helpful.
Q9: Oh absolutely. I downloaded almost all the PDFs and reading materials to my laptop so I could study without being online. I'd print out some notes and study from those while sitting in the library or even on the bus. I also used my own notebook constantly to rewrite concepts in my own words. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were many times I'd spend hours reviewing my printed notes or re-reading downloaded materials without ever touching the LMS. I'd work through problems on paper, quiz myself using flashcards I made, or just sit and think through difficult concepts. None of that would show up anywhere in the system. My offline study time was probably equal to or more than my online time.
Q11: Yes, this was pretty much my standard approach. I'd access a reading or download a video once, then work with it offline for days. I'd annotate the PDFs on my tablet or print them out and highlight them. The LMS would only show that one initial access, but I might have reviewed that same material five or six times on my own. It was more convenient than constantly logging in.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I was taking nine online courses simultaneously, and the workload just piled up. Even though I kept logging in and completing tasks, I felt like I wasn't actually absorbing anything. I was going through the motions but not really understanding. The stress was high, and I think my resilience just wasn't there to push through the confusion.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, I'd still log into the LMS and do the bare minimum to stay on track with tasks. But my engagement was superficial—I'd skim readings instead of really studying them, or I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to mark them complete. The competing demands of nine courses meant I couldn't give any single course the deep attention it probably needed.
Q14: Yeah, more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. I'd realize I'd read the same paragraph three times without processing it. Or I'd watch a video and immediately forget what it covered. I was physically present in the system but mentally checked out.
Q15: I postponed studying for exams more than I should have. I'd focus on completing assignments since they had clear deadlines and felt more manageable. Exam prep felt overwhelming because I didn't know where to start, so I'd put it off. My assignment completion was pretty good, but my exam scores really suffered because of this avoidance pattern.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my entire LMS usage pattern. I'd check the calendar constantly and plan my week around what was due. As deadlines approached, my login frequency would spike and I'd be much more active. Without a deadline looming, I'd still log in out of habit, but my engagement was more casual and less focused.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to keep going with the same approach. But when feedback was critical or my scores were lower than expected, I'd feel discouraged and sometimes avoid that part of the course for a bit. The CBT session I did helped me process negative feedback better without spiraling into avoidance.
Q18: The course structure gave me a clear roadmap, which I appreciated. Having weekly modules with defined tasks made it easy to build a routine around. But with nine courses all structured differently, sometimes the schedules would conflict and I'd have to prioritize one over another. A more predictable structure across all courses would have helped me manage better.
Q19: The discussion forums were designed well and made engagement easy—I liked that participation was expected and counted toward something. What made engagement harder was when exam content didn't clearly align with the weekly materials. I'd complete all the tasks and readings but then face exam questions that felt like they came from nowhere. That disconnect was really frustrating.
Q20: I can totally relate to this pattern. Sometimes you're trying really hard to understand the material—watching videos, reading everything, participating in discussions—but when it comes to actually producing work, you just can't translate that effort into results. The understanding isn't there even though the effort is. High activity doesn't always mean high comprehension.
Q21: That wasn't really my experience since I had high LMS activity. But I can see how someone who studies mostly offline or has strong prior knowledge could do well without logging in much. They might download everything once and master it on their own. The LMS activity doesn't capture what happens in someone's head or in their notebook.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when you logged in, what you clicked on, and whether you submitted things on time. They're good at tracking compliance and surface-level engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you actually understood what you read, how much you struggled, or how much learning happened offline. They capture behavior, not comprehension.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's about being mentally present when you're studying, asking questions when confused, and connecting new information to what you already know. I was engaged in terms of showing up and doing the work, but I'm not sure I was always engaged in terms of deep learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled internally despite appearing active. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent with printed notes, the stress I felt managing nine courses, or how the CBT session actually helped my mindset. It also wouldn't show that my high task completion masked a real problem with actually understanding the exam material. The numbers look okay, but they hide the struggle.
